title: Ops at Scale — Dragon Scales? You Need Enterprise Scale!

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will use Copilot Chat to tackle enterprise-scale operational challenges: generating scripts, analyzing logs, writing runbooks, building monitoring queries, and drafting reliability reports.

This lab is tailored for ops engineers, SREs, platform engineers, and IT leaders. The guide panel will update automatically as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction:
We've opened Copilot Chat for you. This is your primary tool for the rest of the lab. You will use conversational prompts to generate ops artifacts instead of writing them by hand.

Take a moment to confirm the chat panel is visible on the right side of your screen.

steps.title: Explore the AI-Assisted Ops Shift
steps.instruction:
In the Chat panel, type:

What are the top 5 manual operations tasks that AI can help automate for an enterprise SRE team?

Compare Copilot's response with the before-and-after list on the slide. Notice how Copilot organizes its answer; this framing will guide the rest of the lab.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Generate an IaC Pipeline
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

Write a GitHub Actions workflow that runs terraform plan on pull requests and terraform apply on merge to main. Include environment protection rules for production.

Review the generated YAML. Notice how Copilot structures the workflow with triggers, jobs, and steps.

steps.title: Iterate on the Pipeline
steps.instruction:
Now refine the workflow conversationally. Type:

Add a Slack notification step that sends a message on workflow failure

This demonstrates how you can build complex pipelines incrementally by layering requests. Each response builds on the previous context.

steps.title: Add Drift Detection
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to extend the workflow further:

Add a scheduled job that runs nightly to detect infrastructure drift by comparing terraform plan output, and opens a GitHub Issue if drift is found

Review the result. This is the kind of automation that prevents configuration drift from becoming an incident.
steps.tip: Always review generated workflows carefully before committing them. Treat Copilot output like a pull request from a colleague.

steps.title: Save Your Pipeline
steps.instruction:
We've opened a new file for you. Set the language to YAML using the language selector in the bottom-right corner of the editor.

Paste the GitHub Actions workflow from Chat into this file. SREs should always save generated pipelines as reviewable artifacts — never leave useful automation trapped in a chat conversation.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar and the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Generate a Cleanup Script
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

Write a PowerShell script that finds and lists orphaned Azure resources (unattached disks, unused public IPs, empty resource groups). Include a dry-run flag that only reports without deleting.

Review the generated script. Notice the safety features like dry-run mode and logging.

steps.title: Add Guardrails
steps.instruction:
Refine the script by asking:

Add structured logging to a file, a confirmation prompt before any deletions, and error handling with retry logic for API throttling

These guardrails are essential for production scripts. Copilot can layer them in without you rewriting the core logic.

steps.title: Generate a Secret Rotation Script
steps.instruction:
Try a different ops scenario:

Write a Bash script that rotates Azure Key Vault secrets older than 90 days. List expiring secrets first, then prompt for confirmation before rotating each one.

This demonstrates how Copilot handles security-sensitive automation. Always verify that generated scripts follow your organization's security policies.
steps.tip: Store reviewed scripts in a shared ops repository so your entire team can reuse them.

steps.title: Test a Command
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Terminal for you. SREs live in the terminal — this is where your generated scripts will ultimately run.

Try a quick validation command:

echo 'Script validation: dry-run mode active' && date

In practice, you would paste the generated cleanup script here and run it with the dry-run flag first. Always test generated scripts in a non-production environment before scheduling them.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar and the terminal for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Analyze a Log Snippet
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, paste the following simulated log and ask for analysis:

Analyze this log and identify the root cause: 2026-02-26T08:12:01Z ERROR payment-svc Connection pool exhausted, max connections: 50 2026-02-26T08:12:03Z WARN api-gateway Upstream timeout: payment-svc (30s) 2026-02-26T08:12:05Z ERROR api-gateway HTTP 503 returned to client, path: /checkout 2026-02-26T08:12:06Z WARN order-svc Retry attempt 3/3 failed for payment-svc

Review how Copilot identifies the sequence of failures and traces them to the source.

steps.title: Generate a KQL Query
steps.instruction:
Now ask Copilot to generate a query for deeper investigation:

Write a KQL query for Azure Monitor that finds all 5xx errors from the payment service in the last 4 hours, grouped by error code and 15-minute intervals

This is the kind of query you would run in Log Analytics during an active incident.

steps.title: Summarize for Stakeholders
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to translate the technical analysis into a stakeholder-friendly summary:

Summarize the payment service issue from the logs above in 3-4 sentences for a non-technical audience. Include the impact and current status.

This is invaluable during incidents when leadership needs quick updates without the technical details.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Generate a Runbook
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

Generate a runbook in Markdown for handling a database connection pool exhaustion incident. Include these sections: Symptoms, Diagnosis Steps, Resolution Actions, Escalation Paths, and Post-Incident Checklist.

Review the structure. A well-organized runbook reduces mean-time-to-resolve by giving on-call engineers a clear playbook.

steps.title: Review an Existing Runbook
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to act as a reviewer:

Review the runbook you just generated. Flag any sections that might become outdated if we migrate from a self-managed database to a managed database service like Azure SQL. Suggest updates.

This pattern helps keep runbooks current as your architecture evolves. Teams can use this as part of their regular documentation review cycle.
steps.tip: Store runbooks as Markdown in a GitHub repository. Use pull requests to track changes and maintain version history.

steps.title: Save Your Runbook
steps.instruction:
We've opened a new file for you. Set the language to Markdown and paste the runbook from Chat.

A runbook is only useful if it is saved, versioned, and discoverable. In your organization, store runbooks in a shared ops-runbooks repository where on-call engineers can find them during an incident.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar and the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Generate a KQL Monitoring Query
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

Write a KQL query for Azure Monitor that shows the p95 latency for the orders API grouped by region for the last 24 hours. Include a time bin of 1 hour.

Review the generated query. Notice how Copilot uses summarize, percentile, and bin functions correctly.

steps.title: Generate a PromQL Alert Rule
steps.instruction:
Try a different monitoring stack:

Write a Prometheus alert rule in YAML that fires when memory usage exceeds 85% for more than 5 minutes on any production node. Include labels for severity and a meaningful annotation.

Compare the PromQL syntax with the KQL query above. Copilot handles both query languages conversationally.

steps.title: Scaffold a Dashboard
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to create a full dashboard definition:

Create a Grafana dashboard JSON with four panels: CPU usage, memory usage, disk I/O, and network throughput. Add a variable selector for environment (dev, staging, production).

This dashboard-as-code approach means your monitoring definitions are versioned, reviewable, and reproducible alongside your infrastructure.
steps.tip: Standardize dashboard templates across teams so on-call engineers can quickly orient themselves when investigating any service.

steps.title: Review Monitoring Settings
steps.instruction:
We've opened Settings for you. Type output in the search bar to see configuration options for the Output panel and terminal behavior.

In an ops workflow, you would configure settings like terminal.integrated.scrollback (how much log history to keep) and output channel visibility. These small configurations add up to a smoother incident response experience.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar and the Settings tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Settings: Ctrl+,

steps.title: Correlate Alerts
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, paste this simulated alert timeline and ask for correlation:

Which of these alerts are related, and what is the likely root cause? 08:10 - WARN: High CPU on db-primary-01 (92%) 08:11 - CRITICAL: Connection pool exhausted on payment-svc 08:12 - WARN: Upstream timeout on api-gateway to payment-svc 08:12 - CRITICAL: HTTP 503 spike on api-gateway 08:13 - WARN: Queue depth increasing on order-processing 08:14 - CRITICAL: SLA breach on checkout flow (p99 > 5s)

Review how Copilot traces the cascade from database CPU to SLA breach.

steps.title: Generate Remediation Steps
steps.instruction:
Follow up with:

Based on the root cause you identified, generate a step-by-step remediation plan. Include both an immediate fix and a long-term prevention strategy.

Notice how Copilot separates short-term actions (restart, scale up) from long-term improvements (connection pooling, auto-scaling). This structure is exactly what incident commanders need during a response.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Draft a Postmortem
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

Draft a blameless postmortem in Markdown for the following incident: Incident: Payment service outage on 2026-02-26 Duration: 08:10 to 08:45 (35 minutes) Impact: 12% of checkout transactions failed Root cause: Database connection pool exhaustion due to a config change at 08:05 Resolution: Reverted config change and increased pool size Include sections for Summary, Timeline, Root Cause, Impact, Lessons Learned, and Action Items.

Review the structure. A good postmortem captures lessons without assigning blame.

steps.title: Convert Action Items to Issues
steps.instruction:
Follow up with:

Convert the action items from the postmortem into GitHub Issue descriptions. For each issue, include a title, description, acceptance criteria, and a suggested label (bug, enhancement, or infrastructure).

This ensures follow-up work is tracked and completed. In a real workflow, you would create these as actual GitHub Issues with assignees and deadlines.

steps.title: Generate a Stakeholder Summary
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to create a leadership-friendly version:

Write a 4-sentence executive summary of this incident suitable for a weekly ops review meeting. Focus on business impact, resolution time, and prevention measures.

Ops teams often need to communicate upward in non-technical language. Copilot bridges that gap quickly.
steps.tip: Publish postmortems as Markdown files in a GitHub repository. Link them from your incident tracking system for easy reference.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Browse Your Ops Artifacts
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Explorer sidebar. Take a moment to review the files you have created so far in this lab — pipeline YAML, scripts, runbooks, and monitoring configurations.

In a real ops environment, the Explorer is your file navigator for infrastructure-as-code repositories. Keeping artifacts organized and versioned is as important as creating them.

steps.title: Generate an SLO Report
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

Generate a monthly SLO report template in Markdown for a payment service with these objectives: - Availability: 99.95% target - p99 Latency: under 500ms - Error Rate: below 0.1% Include sections for current performance, error budget remaining, trend analysis, and recommended actions.

Review the template. This is the kind of report that builds credibility with stakeholders when generated consistently from real data.

steps.title: Analyze Cloud Spend
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to help with cost analysis:

I have a cloud bill showing these monthly costs: - Compute (VMs): $45,000 (up 20% from last month) - Storage: $12,000 (flat) - Networking: $8,500 (up 35%) - Database: $22,000 (flat) Summarize the top cost drivers, flag the anomalies, and suggest 3 cost optimization actions I should investigate.

This is how Copilot helps you prepare cost-savings proposals for leadership without switching to a dedicated FinOps tool.
steps.tip: Export billing data to a repository on a regular schedule so you can track spending trends over time with version history.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Discover Terminal Profiles
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Command Palette for you. Type terminal select to see commands for managing terminal profiles.

As a platform engineer, you can configure default terminal profiles for your team — ensuring everyone uses the same shell and environment. This is a small governance step that prevents cross-platform scripting issues.

steps.title: Design an Org Structure
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

We are a 500-person engineering org adopting GitHub Enterprise. Recommend an organization structure: should we use a single org or multiple orgs? How should we set base permissions, team structure, and repository visibility to support innersource while meeting compliance requirements?

Review the recommendations. Notice how Copilot balances autonomy with governance, the central tension of enterprise GitHub adoption.

steps.title: Generate a Repository Template Checklist
steps.instruction:
Follow up with:

Create a checklist for a GitHub repository template that all new projects in our org must use. Include CI/CD, security scanning (CodeQL, secret scanning, Dependabot), branch protection rules, CODEOWNERS, and documentation requirements.

This template ensures every new repository starts with best practices baked in, reducing onboarding time and preventing security gaps.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You have completed the Dragon Scales lab. Throughout this session you used Copilot Chat, the Terminal, Explorer, Settings, and the Command Palette to:

• Generate and iterate on IaC pipelines with GitHub Actions
• Create ops scripts and test them in the Terminal
• Analyze logs and generate KQL and PromQL queries
• Write, review, and save runbooks as living documentation
• Scaffold monitoring dashboards and review monitoring settings
• Correlate alerts and generate remediation plans
• Draft blameless postmortems and convert action items to Issues
• Build SLO reports and analyze cloud spend
• Design org structure and repository templates for enterprise scale

Take these patterns back to your team and start with the area that will save you the most time this week.
steps.tip: Head back to the slides on the left for a recap of key takeaways.
